learning_objectives: Articulate the difference between accessibility compliance and inclusive design thinking
Identify permanent, temporary, and situational disabilities across all senses
Apply the Microsoft Inclusive Design principles to product development
readings: Mismatch: How Inclusion Shapes Design by Kat Holmes, Ch. 1-3

lecture_notes.introduction: Inclusive design is not about designing for edge cases — it is about recognizing that every human has abilities that vary across context and time. Designing for the widest range of human diversity benefits everyone.
key_concepts: The persona spectrum: permanent, temporary, and situational disabilities
Inclusive design principles: recognize exclusion, learn from diversity, solve for one to extend to many
The curb cut effect: accessible solutions that improve the experience for everyone
Legal landscape: ADA, Section 508, EAA, and litigation trends
Business case: 1.3 billion people with disabilities represent $8 trillion in spending power
lecture_notes.demonstration: Experiencing exclusion firsthand: using a website with one hand, with simulated low vision, and with a screen reader — then discussing how each experience reveals design failures
setup_requirements: Low vision simulation goggles or browser extension

review.value: One arm amputated (permanent), broken arm in cast (temporary), parent holding infant (situational) — all three need one-handed design. Solving for one extends to all three.

Curb cuts were designed for wheelchair users but benefit everyone: parents with strollers, travelers with luggage, cyclists. Similarly, captions help deaf users, noisy environments, and language learners.

class_schedule.topic: WCAG 2.1 Deep Dive: Perceivable and Operable
learning_objectives: Apply WCAG 2.1 AA success criteria for perceivable and operable content
Design text alternatives, captions, and audio descriptions for media content
Ensure all functionality is keyboard accessible with visible focus indicators

lecture_notes.introduction: WCAG 2.1 Level AA is the standard for digital accessibility compliance. Understanding each principle in depth transforms abstract guidelines into concrete design requirements.
key_concepts: Perceivable: text alternatives, captions, adaptable content, distinguishable presentation
Operable: keyboard accessible, enough time, no seizure triggers, navigable, input modalities
Writing effective alt text: descriptive for informative images, empty for decorative
Focus management: visible focus indicators, logical focus order, focus trapping in modals
Motion: prefers-reduced-motion respect, no auto-playing animation without controls
lecture_notes.demonstration: Auditing a real web page against every WCAG 2.1 AA criterion for Perceivable and Operable, documenting each pass and failure
setup_requirements: axe DevTools and WAVE browser extensions

review.value: Informative images: describe the content and function ('Graph showing 30% revenue growth in Q3'). Decorative images: use empty alt='' so screen readers skip them. Never use 'image of' — the screen reader already announces it is an image.

WCAG 2.4.7 requires visible focus indicators. The default browser outline is functional but often removed by designers. Replace with a custom focus style — at least 2px solid with sufficient contrast — never remove without replacement.

class_schedule.topic: Screen Reader Design and ARIA Patterns
learning_objectives: Design interfaces that provide a logical screen reader experience
Apply ARIA roles, states, and properties to custom interactive components
Test designs with screen readers and interpret the spoken output
readings: ARIA Authoring Practices Guide (APG) by W3C
Inclusive Components by Heydon Pickering, Ch. on Tabs and Menus

lecture_notes.introduction: Screen readers do not see your visual design. They read the DOM structure, semantic HTML, and ARIA attributes. If your design is only visual, it is invisible to screen reader users.
key_concepts: Semantic HTML first: heading levels, landmarks, lists, forms
ARIA when semantics are insufficient: roles, states, properties
The five rules of ARIA: use native HTML first, do not change native semantics, make all interactive ARIA controls keyboard operable
Common ARIA patterns: tabs, accordions, modals, comboboxes, live regions
Live regions: aria-live for dynamic content updates without page refresh
lecture_notes.demonstration: Building an accessible tab interface using ARIA roles and keyboard controls — testing with VoiceOver at each step to hear how screen readers interpret the markup

review.value: (1) Use native HTML when possible. (2) Do not change native element semantics. (3) All ARIA controls must be keyboard operable. (4) Do not use role='presentation' on focusable elements. (5) All interactive elements must have accessible names.

Announces dynamic content changes to screen readers without focus change — aria-live='polite' waits for idle, aria-live='assertive' interrupts. Essential for notifications, search results, and real-time updates.

learning_objectives: Design for users with cognitive disabilities: ADHD, dyslexia, autism, brain injuries
Apply plain language principles to reduce cognitive load in interfaces
Create predictable, consistent interfaces that support memory and attention challenges
readings: COGA (Cognitive and Learning Disabilities Accessibility) Task Force guidelines

lecture_notes.introduction: Cognitive accessibility is the most underserved area of inclusive design. Approximately 10-15% of the population has a cognitive or learning disability. Design decisions around language, consistency, and predictability are critical.
key_concepts: Cognitive load types: intrinsic, extraneous, germane — minimize extraneous
Plain language: short sentences, common words, active voice, clear structure
Predictable behavior: consistent navigation, no unexpected changes, clear cause-effect
Memory support: breadcrumbs, recent items, saved state, progress indicators
Error prevention: constraints, confirmations, forgiving input formats
lecture_notes.demonstration: Rewriting a complex legal terms-of-service page using plain language principles — before and after readability scores and user comprehension testing

review.value: Mental effort wasted on figuring out the interface rather than accomplishing the task — caused by inconsistent navigation, unclear labels, hidden information, and unpredictable behavior. Reduce by removing friction, not content.
Flesch-Kincaid Grade Level 6-8 for general audience. Short sentences (under 20 words). One idea per paragraph. Common words over jargon. Active voice over passive.

learning_objectives: Conduct comprehensive accessibility audits using automated and manual methods
Prioritize accessibility issues by user impact and effort to fix
Create accessibility remediation roadmaps for product teams
readings: A Web for Everyone by Sarah Horton & Whitney Quesenbery, Ch. 8-10

lecture_notes.introduction: Automated tools catch approximately 30% of accessibility issues. The remaining 70% require manual testing with assistive technologies and human judgment. A complete audit combines both approaches.
key_concepts: Automated testing: axe, Lighthouse, WAVE — what they catch and what they miss
Manual testing checklist: keyboard navigation, screen reader, zoom, color contrast
User testing with people with disabilities: recruiting, facilitation, compensation
Accessibility remediation prioritization: user impact, frequency, effort
Building accessibility into the design process vs. retrofitting after launch
lecture_notes.demonstration: Full accessibility audit of a class-selected website: automated scan, keyboard walkthrough, screen reader testing, and documented findings with prioritized remediation plan

review.value: Automated tools catch ~30% of WCAG violations (missing alt text, contrast failures, missing labels). Manual testing catches the other ~70% (logical focus order, meaningful alt text, screen reader flow, cognitive issues).

Integrating accessibility into design (left of development) costs 10x less than retrofitting after launch. Designers annotating focus order, heading levels, and alt text in mockups prevents 60%+ of accessibility defects.

questions.question: Apply the persona spectrum concept to the ability to 'see a screen clearly.' Identify one permanent, one temporary, and one situational example, then describe one design solution that helps all three.
questions.answer: Permanent: a user who is legally blind with limited central vision. Temporary: a user recovering from eye surgery with dilated pupils. Situational: a user trying to read their phone in bright sunlight. Design solution that helps all three: high-contrast text (meeting WCAG AAA 7:1 ratio) with a large default font size (18px+) and support for system font size scaling. High contrast helps the legally blind user read with magnification, helps the post-surgery user read through light sensitivity, and helps the outdoor user read through glare. Additional: a dark mode option serves all three differently — low vision with high contrast, post-surgery with reduced brightness, outdoor use with better screen legibility.

rubric.key: One permanent, one temporary, one situational — all correct
Design solution genuinely helps all three groups

questions.question: A website uses a custom toggle switch built with a div element. It looks like a standard toggle but has no keyboard support, no screen reader label, and no visual focus indicator. List the WCAG success criteria this violates and explain the fix for each.
questions.answer: Violations: (1) WCAG 2.1.1 Keyboard — not operable via keyboard. Fix: add tabindex='0' and handle Space and Enter key events to toggle. (2) WCAG 4.1.2 Name, Role, Value — no accessible name or role. Fix: add role='switch', aria-checked='true/false', and aria-label describing what the toggle controls. (3) WCAG 2.4.7 Focus Visible — no visible focus indicator. Fix: add a visible focus ring (:focus-visible) with at least 2px solid outline with sufficient contrast. (4) WCAG 1.3.1 Info and Relationships — the on/off state is only communicated visually. Fix: aria-checked communicates state to assistive technology.

rubric.key: Identifies name/role (4.1.2) violation with ARIA fix
Identifies focus visible (2.4.7) violation with fix
Identifies state communication (1.3.1) violation with fix

quizzes.topic: Screen Reader Design and Cognitive Accessibility

questions.question: A modal dialog opens when a user clicks a 'Delete Account' button, but the screen reader continues reading the page content behind the modal. Explain what ARIA pattern is missing and describe the complete accessible modal implementation.
questions.answer: The missing pattern is ARIA modal focus trapping. Complete accessible modal implementation: (1) Add role='dialog' and aria-modal='true' to the modal container. (2) Set aria-labelledby pointing to the modal's heading. (3) When the modal opens, move focus to the first interactive element inside the modal (or the modal itself). (4) Trap focus within the modal — Tab and Shift+Tab cycle through modal content only, not the page behind it. (5) Add aria-hidden='true' to all content outside the modal to prevent screen reader access. (6) On close (Escape key or close button), return focus to the element that triggered the modal (the 'Delete Account' button). (7) Remove aria-hidden from page content when the modal closes.

rubric.key: Identifies focus trapping as the core issue
Explains focus management (into modal, trapped, return on close)

questions.question: Rewrite this error message for cognitive accessibility: 'Error 422: Unprocessable Entity — The server was unable to process the request due to semantic errors in the submitted form data.' Explain what plain language principles you applied.
questions.answer: Rewritten: 'Some information needs to be corrected. Please check the highlighted fields below and try again.' Plain language principles applied: (1) Removed technical jargon — error codes and HTTP terminology mean nothing to users. (2) Used active voice and direct address — 'Please check' tells the user what to do next. (3) Short sentence — under 15 words per sentence for easy scanning. (4) Action-oriented — focuses on the recovery path, not the technical problem. (5) Positive framing — 'needs to be corrected' rather than 'failed' or 'error.' The message answers the three questions every error message should answer: What happened (some information needs correcting), Why (implicit — form data issues), and What to do (check highlighted fields and try again).

rubric.key: Message provides a clear recovery action
Explains specific plain language principles applied
Mentions the three-part error message structure

major_assignments.title: Comprehensive Accessibility Audit and Redesign
major_assignments.description: Conduct a full accessibility audit of a live website, document all WCAG 2.1 AA violations, create a prioritized remediation roadmap, and redesign the three most impactful screens to meet accessibility standards.
objectives: Execute a professional accessibility audit combining automated and manual testing
Document findings using standard accessibility reporting formats
Prioritize issues by user impact and create a remediation roadmap
Redesign key screens to meet WCAG 2.1 AA with inclusive design improvements
deliverables: Accessibility audit report covering all WCAG 2.1 AA criteria (automated + manual)
Issue tracker with all violations: criterion, severity, location, fix description
Prioritized remediation roadmap with effort estimates and sequencing
Redesigned screens (3 key pages) meeting WCAG 2.1 AA with annotated accessibility specs
Screen reader walkthrough recording of the redesigned screens

criteria: All four POUR principles systematically evaluated
Both automated and manual testing methods used
Keyboard navigation fully tested and documented
Screen reader testing completed and documented
Each issue references specific WCAG success criteria
Evidence provided for each finding (screenshots, recordings)
Severity ratings are consistent and justified
Professional report format suitable for client delivery
Prioritization reflects real user impact
Effort estimates are realistic
Quick wins identified separately from larger initiatives
Roadmap is actionable for a development team
Redesigns genuinely meet WCAG 2.1 AA criteria
Accessibility annotations include focus order, heading levels, ARIA needs
Visual design maintains quality while improving accessibility
Screen reader walkthrough demonstrates the improved experience
Thorough audit covering all WCAG 2.1 AA criteria with evidence
Professional documentation with actionable remediation roadmap
High-quality redesigns meeting accessibility standards
Screen reader walkthrough demonstrating real improvement

weekly_quizzes.description: Quizzes on inclusive design principles, WCAG criteria, and assistive technology

accessibility_labs.description: Weekly labs practicing screen reader testing, ARIA patterns, and accessibility auditing

audit_and_redesign.description: Comprehensive accessibility audit and redesign of a live website

assistive_tech_journal.description: Weekly journal documenting personal experience using assistive technologies for daily tasks

final_presentation.description: Presentation of audit findings and redesign to accessibility professionals

course_policies.attendance: All lab sessions are mandatory. Screen reader proficiency requires practice time that cannot be compressed. More than 2 absences will affect your lab and participation grades.
course_policies.late_submissions: Audit phases have sequential deadlines. Late work penalized 10% per day. The final report deadline is firm.
course_policies.collaboration: Lab exercises may be paired. Audit and redesign projects are individual. Sharing audit findings or templates between students is prohibited.
course_policies.academic_integrity: Audit findings must be from your own testing. Copying WCAG failure descriptions from automated tools without personal verification and context is insufficient.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Screen reader (VoiceOver or NVDA — must achieve basic proficiency), axe DevTools, WAVE, Colour Contrast Analyser, keyboard-only navigation proficiency, and Figma.
